title: Chapter 8: Sublevel B
content: Thirty-six hours.

Chloe hadn't slept. Not really. She'd drifted in and out of consciousness in the Airbnb's sagging armchair while her scripts crawled through Aeterna's internal architecture, mapping nodes and permissions and forgotten directories the way a cartographer maps a coastline — one careful sounding at a time. The hum of her server rack rental, piped in through her headphones, had become a kind of white noise. A companion. The only sound in the apartment besides Maya's breathing from the next room and the occasional bark of a dog somewhere in the Daly City grid.

Maya had made her eat. Granola bars, a microwaved burrito, an apple that was more bruise than fruit. Chloe accepted the offerings the way someone accepts oxygen — mechanically, grateful, already forgetting the gesture the moment it passed. Her eyes were bloodshot. Her fingers moved across the keyboard like they belonged to someone else.
And then, at 2:47 AM on what was technically Thursday morning, she found it.
"Maya."
The word came out hoarse, cracked. Maya was asleep on the couch, one arm draped over her face to block the light from Chloe's monitors. She stirred.
"What."
"Get up."

Maya recognized the tone. It was the same tone Chloe had used in the kitchen of the Mission apartment three days ago, when she'd first cracked the Kapoor email. Flat. Controlled. Underneath it: something close to terror.

Maya swung her legs off the couch and crossed to Chloe's workstation in four steps. The three monitors painted her face in blue light. On the center screen: a schematic. Dense, architectural, rendered in the sterile linework of CAD software. It looked like a floor plan for a hospital, or a lab, or—
"What am I looking at?" Maya asked.
"Aeterna's campus filings list two sublevels," Chloe said. "Sublevel A is infrastructure — HVAC, electrical, water treatment. Standard stuff. It's in the public permit applications."
"Sublevel B isn't."

"Sublevel B isn't." Chloe clicked. The left monitor refreshed, showing a directory tree. "It wasn't in any filing. I found it in an encrypted backup on one of their internal dev servers. Someone forgot to rotate the key rotation schedule — they updated the encryption protocol for the production environment but left the staging backup on the old key pair. Amateur hour."
"How amateur are we talking?"
"I'm talking I cracked it with a key I found in a readme file, Maya. A readme file. Somebody at Aeterna was very sloppy or very arrogant, and I'm not sure which one scares me more."
The right monitor showed a document header. Maya leaned in. The text was sparse: PROJECT CARMILLA — SUBLEVEL B ARCHITECTURE — REV 7.3 — CLASSIFIED: EXECUTIVE ACCESS ONLY.

"Project Carmilla," Maya repeated. The name meant nothing to her and everything at the same time — a distant bell, some half-remembered literary reference she couldn't place and didn't have time to chase.
"Look at the layout," Chloe said.

Maya looked. The schematic on the center screen was a top-down view of a subterranean floor. It was elliptical in shape — roughly two hundred feet on its long axis, a hundred and twenty on the short. The space was divided into distinct zones, each labeled with clinical precision. Along the eastern edge: INTAKE ASSESSMENT. Along the western: PROCESSING CORRIDOR. And in the center, dominating the plan like the nucleus of a cell, a large circular space marked CENTRAL FILTRATION HUB.
Radiating out from that hub in two concentric rings: thirty-two smaller rooms, each one identical in size and shape. Each labeled MEDICAL BAY 1 through MEDICAL BAY 32.
"Thirty-two medical bays," Maya said slowly. "What kind of medical bays?"

Chloe zoomed in on one. The detail expanded. The room was roughly ten by twelve feet. Against the far wall: a reclining chair — not a hospital bed, a chair, articulated at multiple joints, with armrests that extended into stabilization brackets. Above the chair: a ceiling-mounted array labeled SMART MONITORING SYSTEM — VITALS / O2 SAT / HEMATOLOGY. On the chair's left arm: three IV ports, color-coded. On the right: a blood collection station with a centrifugal separator. A small desk. A screen. Nothing else.
No window. No personal storage. Nothing that suggested a person lived here.
Only that a person was processed here.
"Chloe."
"I know."
"What is this?"

Chloe zoomed out. The full layout reappeared — the rings of bays, the central hub, the processing corridor. From the hub, a single wide passage led west to a large rectangular space that dominated the entire western quadrant of the sublevel. It was labeled, simply and without elaboration, LONG-TERM STORAGE.

Maya stared at those three words. Long-term storage. Storage of equipment, storage of supplies, storage of data — any of those would make sense in a medical facility. But there was nothing in the blueprint that suggested equipment or supplies or data. The bays had no recovery rooms. The intake area had no discharge protocol. The processing corridor led in one direction only — toward storage.
"Long-term storage of what?" Maya asked.
Chloe didn't answer immediately. Her fingers trembled on the edge of the desk. When she spoke, her voice was barely above a whisper.
"Women." She swallowed. "They're storing women, Maya."

The blueprint stared up at them from the screen, and it was the kind of thing that reorganizes your understanding of the world — the way a microscope slide reorganizes your understanding of water. One moment it's a glass of something clear and harmless. The next it's teeming.

Maya studied the layout with the systematic attention she'd once brought to crime scene photos during her criminology seminars at SF State. The concentric rings weren't decorative. They were functional. The inner ring — Bays 1 through 16 — were closest to the central filtration hub. The outer ring — Bays 17 through 32 — had longer piping runs, which meant they were secondary. Less efficient. The kind of bays you used when the first-choice bays were full.

And the filtration hub itself. She made Chloe pull up the equipment manifest that was appended to the blueprint, Revision 7.3. The hub contained industrial-scale blood processing units: multistage centrifuges, plasma separators, pathogen reduction systems, cold-chain storage for processed product. Each unit had a throughput rating. Maya did the math on the numbers and felt something cold settle into the pit of her stomach.
The system was sized to process the output of all thirty-two bays. Simultaneously. Continuously.

She thought of Kapoor's email. The one that had started all of this. *Specimen #12 is exhibiting signs of hemocytic decline. I strongly recommend reducing the extraction schedule from biweekly to monthly for all Category C subjects.* Hemocytic decline. Reduced extraction schedule. Category C subjects.
Not patients. Subjects. Not treated. Extracted.
"Chloe," Maya said. Her voice was very steady. "Tell me what 'specimen viability' means in the context of a filtration hub."

Chloe pulled up Kapoor's email chain — the one they'd intercepted days ago, the one that had sent them running to this Airbnb in the first place. She cross-referenced the terminology against the equipment manifest.

"The centrifuges separate whole blood into components — red cells, plasma, platelets," Chloe said. Each word came out like she was handling broken glass. "The plasma goes to cold storage. The red cells go through pathogen reduction and then — and this is where it gets —" She stopped. Pressed her lips together. "Then they're categorized. By viability. Kapoor's email mentions three categories: A, B, and C. A is optimal. B is acceptable. C is—"
"Declining," Maya finished. "Because they've been drained too long."
"Yeah."

It was a dairy farm. That was the thought that shoved its way into Maya's brain and refused to leave. A dairy farm. Designed by engineers. Optimized for throughput. Cows in stalls, milk in pipes, product in cold storage. The only difference was the cattle were human, the product was blood, and the farmer was Alexander Vlad.
Maya stood up so fast the chair rolled backward and hit the wall.

She didn't say anything. She walked past Chloe's station, through the kitchenette with its stack of takeout containers and its half-empty Brita pitcher, and shoved open the sliding glass door to the balcony. The night air hit her — cold, salt-tinged, carrying the faint brine and diesel smell of the Daly City coast. She gripped the metal railing with both hands. The metal was ice-cold and slightly damp with fog. It bit into her palms.
She breathed. One breath. Two.

Her stomach lurched. She leaned over the railing and dry-heaved — a long, wracking spasm that produced nothing but a thin string of bile and the sour taste of granola bar at the back of her throat. Her eyes watered. The lights of the apartment complex across the parking lot smeared into blurring streaks.

It wasn't the gore. There was no gore. The blueprint was bloodless — literally bloodless, sanitized into lines and labels and throughput ratings. That was the thing. That was what made her body revolt with such mechanical force. Not the fact of violence but the geometry of it. The efficiency. Someone — multiple someones, because a project this large required architects, contractors, biomedical engineers, project managers — had sat in a conference room with whiteboards and Post-it notes and optimized a system for harvesting human blood.

They'd made design decisions. Reclining chair or hospital bed? Chair — easier to stabilize, easier to access the arms. Three IV ports or four? Three — the fourth doesn't increase yield enough to justify the cost. Smart monitoring system? Yes — reduces staff requirements, allows centralized oversight, generates data for further optimization.

Someone had run a cost-benefit analysis on whether to include windows in the medical bays. Someone had decided no. Someone had drawn a line on a blueprint and labeled it LONG-TERM STORAGE and then gone to lunch.

Maya straightened. Wiped her mouth with the back of her hand. The night was quiet except for the distant hum of Highway 1 and the low mechanical breathing of the apartment building's HVAC system. The fog was thick tonight — it pressed against the railing like something curious, something wanting to see inside.

She thought of Lucy. Lucy in one of those chairs. Lucy's arm extended, IV ports connected, ceiling-mounted monitor tracking the slow decline of her hemocytic values month after month. Lucy, who had sent a signal through her Apple Watch because it was the only communications device they'd let her keep — the one thing they hadn't thought to confiscate because who would use a fitness tracker to send a distress call?

The rage came like a white flash. Not hot. Cold. Clean. So pure it erased everything else — the nausea, the fear, the exhaustion — and left only a single crystalline imperative in its place: *Find her. Get her out. Burn it down.*
Maya's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against the cold railing until the trembling stopped.
Then she went back inside.
Chloe had pulled up a new window. The security schematic for Sublevel B.

"Biometric access only," she said before Maya could speak. "Fingerprint and retinal scan, dual-factor. No keycards, no PINs. The system is air-gapped from the main corporate network — meaning there's no external connection point. No internet, no intranet bridge, no wireless."
"Can you get in remotely?"

"No." The word was final. Chloe didn't say it with frustration; she said it with the flat authority of someone who understood the limits of her own craft. "Air-gapped means air-gapped. No remote exploit in the world works if there's no wire to reach through. I could spend a year on this and never touch Sublevel B from outside."
"So how does anyone access it?"

"Through a terminal inside Sublevel B itself. The system authenticates locally — the biometric data is stored on a secure server inside the sublevel. Or —" Chloe clicked to another tab. A directory with Vlad's executive access permissions. "— from Vlad's personal office. He has a direct terminal with Sublevel B access. Encrypted tunnel. But his office is on the top floor of headquarters, behind three layers of executive security."
"Which means the only way to access Sublevel B digitally is to be physically inside Sublevel B. Or inside Vlad's office."
"Correct."
Maya sat down on the arm of the couch. The leather creaked under her weight. "So we need someone on the inside."
Chloe was quiet for a moment. Then: "Kapoor."
"Kapoor."

"She has Sublevel B access. I found her biometric registration in the security logs — she's cleared for Sublevel B, Sublevel A, and the executive medical wing. She's one of maybe six people who can physically enter that space."
"If she cooperates."

"If she cooperates." Chloe met Maya's eyes. "She searched for FBI whistleblower protection at three in the morning, Maya. She's scared. She wants out. But wanting out and being willing to walk into a federal building are two different things. People who want out spend years wanting. It's the doing that's hard."

Maya heard the weight of experience in Chloe's voice. Not personal experience — Chloe had never been inside anything like Aeterna — but the experience of reading about people who had. Sources, whistle-blowers, the terrified insiders who reached out to journalists and then went silent. Chloe understood the arithmetic of courage: that it wasn't a fixed quantity but a daily expenditure, and some people simply ran out.
"We need to reach her," Maya said. "Properly. Not just intercepted search queries."
"Agreed. But that's a conversation for later. Because there's something else."

Chloe minimized the security schematic and pulled up the Apple Watch data. Maya's heart did something it had been doing a lot lately — a kind of arrhythmic stutter, like an engine misfiring. Lucy's watch. The only thread connecting them to a woman who might already be dead.

"A new signal came through overnight," Chloe said. "Around 1:15 AM. The transmission was weaker this time — shorter burst, lower power. Like she's being more careful. Or like she's moved somewhere with worse signal."
"Decode it."
Chloe ran the extraction script. The garbled data resolved into text, letter by letter, appearing on screen with the agonizing slowness of a message assembled from noise:
NEW SHIPMENT COMING IPO DAY
THEYRE PREPPING ALL BAYS
MOVE ME WITH THE OTHERS IF YOU CAN
Maya read it three times. The words didn't change.

New shipment. They were bringing more women in. On IPO day — twenty-six days from now, the single most watched event in Aeterna's corporate history, the day the company would go public and Alexander Vlad's net worth would graduate from astronomical to whatever came after astronomical. All eyes on the stock price. All cameras on the CEO. And in the basement, every bay filled.
"They're going to fill every bay," Maya said. Not a question.

"IPO day is the perfect cover," Chloe said. Her voice had that brittle, controlled quality again — someone talking fast so they don't have to feel. "The campus will be crawling with external visitors — investors, press, event staff. Security will be focused on the public floors. Nobody's going to notice thirty-two women being processed in a sublevel that officially doesn't exist."
"The IPO isn't a financial event. It's a harvest."

The word hung in the air between them. Harvest. The most ancient word for the most ancient transaction. Take what grows. Process what bleeds. Store what survives. The blueprint suddenly made a different kind of sense — not as a hospital or a lab but as a threshing floor. Project Carmilla. The name clicked into place in Maya's mind, and this time she recognized the reference: Sheridan Le Fanu, 1872, the Gothic novella about a female vampire who preys on young women in the drawing rooms of aristocratic Europe. Carmilla. Someone at Aeterna had a sense of humor.

Maya looked at the Apple Watch message again. *Move me with the others if you can.* Lucy was asking for help. Not begging — Lucy never begged, not in any of the messages they'd decoded — but asking. With the specific, clipped clarity of someone who had learned to communicate in fragments because fragments were all the surveillance allowed.
Twenty-six days. Thirty-two bays. A new shipment.

Maya sat with it. She sat with it the way you sit with a diagnosis — not fighting it, not processing it, just letting it occupy the space it demanded. The Airbnb was quiet. Chloe's server hummed. Somewhere in the building, a pipe clanked twice and went silent.
Then Maya said something that surprised even herself.
"I need to apply for that job."
Chloe looked up from her monitors. "What job?"

"The AV Technician position. At Aeterna. It's been listed on their careers page for two weeks — I checked when we first started looking into the company. It's a junior role, event support, campus-wide access. They need someone to run the audio-visual setups for the IPO roadshow events. Town halls. Investor presentations." Maya's voice was calm. Too calm. The calm of someone who has skipped past the part where they're scared and arrived at the part where they're certain. "It gives me campus access. I can get into areas you can't reach remotely. I can plant devices. I can map the physical security. I can find the entrance to Sublevel B."
"You'd be walking into a vampire's lair." Chloe's voice was barely a whisper.
"I know."
"Maya—"

"He doesn't know I exist." Maya stood up. Her reflection ghosted across the dark monitor — dark eyes, sharp jaw, the face of a woman who had spent the last three years behind a microphone asking questions that made powerful men uncomfortable. "He doesn't know Lucy has friends. He doesn't know anyone is watching. He thinks she's alone. He thinks they're all alone."
Her hand found the back of the chair. Gripped it. Released.
"She's not."
Chloe's face was a war — respect fighting fear, strategy fighting terror. She opened her mouth. Closed it. Opened it again. What came out wasn't an objection.
"I'll build you a kit. Earpiece. Camera. Signal relay. If you're going in, you're going in wired."

Maya nodded. She pulled her phone from her pocket. The screen glowed in the dark room — email, weather, a notification from her podcast host reminding her that Episode 1 was due for final edit. She navigated to Safari. Typed: *Aeterna Technologies careers*.
The page loaded. The logo appeared — a sleek serif A with a blood-red accent dot. Below it: a list of open positions. Third from the top: AV Technician — Events & Media — On-site, Menlo Park.
Maya's thumb hovered over the APPLY button.
The room hummed. Chloe's server. The HVAC. Something else — the frequency of a decision already made reverberating in the small space between thought and action.
Her thumb pressed down.
